## [00:00 - 03:09]

So let's just jump right into that. So here you can see the overview of the whole system, right? We are making batches of 50 prospects. Now the number here is sort of just an average. If you're doing 50 connection requests a day, on average you're going to get between 50 and 100, maybe a little more people per week. And we basically do this on a weekly basis, right? So every week at the end of the week we add all of the people who've connected with us into one of these batches and then they get put into this nurture system. And here's how we've set up the cadence. I do recommend that you follow this cadence. If you message more quickly or if you send out these messages more quickly, people are going to feel like it's spammy. This does not, however, mean that you should not message back and forth with the people. In fact, that is one of the primary goals that we have for this campaign is to foster communication back and forth between that person the more the better. Sometimes a conversation that you have with them will lead to them wanting a call or something like that. And then you want to pull them out of the campaign unless you just want to send them over the content. But it can be pretty tough to track who needs to not get a request for a call after you've done one. So our best practice is, hey, if you have a call with this person, pull them out of the campaign and that will ensure that they don't receive messages that appear to be automated because relationally you're taking a step backwards by asking them for a call when you already have one. Okay, enough about that. So let's have an overview here. We have our prospects batches over here and these are set up into these groups. And then here is the message sequence that we have and we've highlighted the ones where we're actually making an ask from them. The messages aren't making a withdrawal from the relational bank account.

## [03:09 - 06:17]

They're deposits. Now that being said, it is important to try to write very short messages for these that promote a response. Getting a response is one of the key elements we found in generating potential business opportunities because it gets them to interact with you and you very quickly become more than just another LinkedIn connection, you become somebody that is on their radar with your business expertise in whatever niche that you're in. ## [06:17 - 09:23]

You can enter the number of search criteria that you used here, which corresponds back to the search criteria page. You have the dates that these are sent, which message you sent that day, total number you sent, which is 50 if you're following our advice. The next day, you're counting how many people actually accept it. This will give you the conversion rate. We should put in a conversion rate calculator over here. That's just number sent to accepted. Here is where you're going to want to put the batches. In each batch, you're going to have the LinkedIn URL for each person. You're just going to want to put all of those in here. There are tools that make it easy to grab programmatic data, structured data from LinkedIn so you can copy paste all the URLs from a LinkedIn search into this document. I just set up the process and copy paste one by one as I go. There are automation options out there, but I don't recommend them to anybody. The reason for that is because it's definitely gray hat at that point. If you're automating LinkedIn, it's against LinkedIn's terms of service. I can't really recommend that other people break LinkedIn's terms of service because that could result in account termination. As a coach, I'm not going to recommend something that puts you in an awkward position where LinkedIn could literally ban you from the platform. That would be terrible for me. I would feel terrible. It would be terrible for you if that happened to you. While I realize these solutions exist and I have tested many of them myself on my own LinkedIn profile, I'm not going to tell you to do that. If you do decide to do that, I would just advise that you use caution. One of the ways that LinkedIn detects these systems is through the Chrome browser. What I'm getting at here is that many of these tools are Chrome extensions. You can tell what Chrome extensions you're using when you log into LinkedIn and you have them on there. They're able to detect code on the page that you've loaded. They do know how to figure out who is using what Chrome extension on their platform.

## [09:23 - 10:50]

If you do look to automate some of this, I urge you to avoid Chrome extension-based tools because of that. There are other tools out there that have been architected in a different way that either are standalone software tools on your PC or Mac or they create a backend server and they kind of create their own virtual browser behind the scenes. If you are going to automate, those are the tools that I recommend that you use because it's much more difficult for LinkedIn to tell the difference between a person automating and not when there's no Chrome extension.
